content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 15, 1992

Laury L. Dowd
Senior Deputy City Attorney
801 11th Street
P.O. Box 642
Modesto, CA 95353

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our Files Nos. I-92-369
through I-92-375

Dear Ms. Dowd:

This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the duties and responsibilities of City of Modesto Mayor
Richard Lang, Councilmembers Ken Friedman, Frank Muratore,
Charles Bird, David Cogdill and Stan Dobbs, and City Manager Ed
Tewes under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act"). Your letter advises not seek advice
regarding a specific pending governmental decision but rather
seeks guidance with respect to various pending decisions.
Accordingly, we treat your letter as a request for informal
assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.2

QUESTIONS

Under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, may the
public officials on whose behalf you seek our advice participate
in governmental decisions regarding projects situated beyond a
radius of 300 feet from their interests in real property?

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 2

1. May Mayor Richard Lang participate in decisions to instal
a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and Leveland
Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift station
at Ashford Court?

2. May Councilmembers Bird and Codgill participate in
decisions to repair city parking lots, perform a structural
evaluation of the annex to the city hall, replace a sewer and
abate asbestos at the Maddux building? May they participate in
decisions to install a bridge to connect Lincoln and Lakewood
Avenues, restripe Scenic Drive and El Vista Drive, and add traffic
improvements at Scenic Avenue and Oakdale Avenue? May they
participate in decisions to perform maintenance overlay work on
Oakdale Road, install drinking fountains and make improvements in
the existing clubhouse at the Creekside Golf Course, and complete
McClure House site improvements?

3. May Councilmember Stan Dobb participate in decisions to
reconstruct a parking lot at Beyer Park and to restripe Scenic
Drive?

4. May Councilmember Frank Muratore participate in decisions
to restripe Tully, modify the existing traffic signal at College
and Orangeburg Avenues, build a bus shelter on Tully and Coldwell,
and abate asbestos at the Coldwell city offices?

5. May Councilmember Kenni Friedman participate in decisions
to instal a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and
Leveland Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift
station at Ashford Court?

6. May City Manager Ed Tewes participate in decisions
regarding construction of a bridge to connect Lincoln and Lakewood
Avenues?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Mayor Richard Lang may participate in decisions to instal
a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and Leveland
Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift station
at Ashford Court unless you determine that decisions regarding
these projects will have a material financial effect on the
mayor's economic interests.

2. Councilmembers Bird and Codgill may participate in
decisions to repair city parking lots, perform a structural
evaluation of the annex to the city hall, replace a sewer and
abate asbestos at the Maddux building, install a bridge to connect
Lincoln and Lakewood Avenues, restripe Scenic Drive and El Vista
Drive, add traffic improvements at Scenic Avenue and Oakdale
Avenue, perform maintenance overlay work on Oakdale Road, instal
drinking fountains and make improvements in the existing clubhouse
at the Creekside Golf Course, and complete McClure House site

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 3

improvements unless you determine that these decisions will have a
material financial effect on their economic interests.

     3.  Councilmember Stan Dobb may participate in decisions to
reconstruct a parking lot at Beyer Park and to restripe Scenic
Drive unless you determine that decisions regarding these projects
will have a material financial effect on his economic interests.

     4.  Councilmember Frank Muratore may participate in decisions
to restripe Tully, modify the existing traffic signal at College
and Orangeburg Avenues, build a bus shelter on Tully and Coldwell,
and abate asbestos at the Coldwell city offices unless you
determine that decisions regarding these projects will have a
material financial effect on his economic interests.

     5.  Councilmember Kenni Friedman may participate in decisions
to instal a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and
Leveland Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift
station at Ashford Court unless you determine that decisions
regarding these projects will have a material financial effect on
her interests in real property.

     6.  City Manager Ed Tewes may participate in decisions
regarding construction of a bridge to connect Lincoln and Lakewood
Avenues unless you determine that this project will have a
material financial effect on his interests in real property.

FACTS

     The City of Modesto is in the process of implementing various
projects. You seek our advice to determine whether seven public
officials who own real property within the jurisdiction must
disqualify themselves from participating in governmental decisions
regarding these projects. All of these projects are situated
beyond a radius of 300 feet from the public officials' interests
in real property.

Interests in real property

Mayor Richard Lang

     Mayor Lang has an interest in his residence which exceeds
$100,000. The mayor also owns two investment properties located
at 2604 and 2613 MacGregor. His interest in these properties
exceeds $10,000. These investment properties consist of rental
units. The mayor's interests in real property are located within
2,500 feet of projects #1 and #2.3


     3 The projects are listed below in numerical order for purposes
of convenience in proceeding with our analysis.

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 4

Councilmember Charles Bird

Councilmember Bird has an interest which is greater than
$1,000 in his residence situated at 3308 Kings Point Road. He
also has a 1/3 interest in investment property situated at 701 H
Street. The fair market value of this real property exceeds
$100,000. Mr. Bird's interests in real property are situated
within 2,500 feet of projects #3, #4, #5, #6, #8, #12, and #13.
Mr. Bird's residence is beyond 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of
project #7.

Councilmember Dave Cogdill

Councilmember Cogdill has an interest greater than $1,000 in
his residence situated at 2505 Stonebrook Drive. He also has an
interest in investment property situated at 1317 I Street. His
ownership interest in this property exceeds ten percent and the
fair market value of the building exceeds $100,000. Mr. Cogdill's
interests in real property are situated within 2,500 feet of
projects #3, #4, #5, #6, #8, #9, #10, #11, #12, and #13.
Mr. Cogdill's residence is beyond a 2,500 foot radius of project
#7.

Councilmember Stan Dobbs

Councilmember Dobbs has an interest greater than $1,000 in
his residence situated at 2629 Portsmouth Lane. The residence is
within a 2,500 foot radius of project #8. He also has an interest
in investment property situated at 3400 Kingswood Drive #12. His
interest in this property is greater than 10 percent and the fair
market value of the property is estimated to be between $10,000
and $100,000. This investment property is within a 2,500 radius
of project #14.

Councilmember Frank Muratore

Councilmember Muratore has an interest in his residence
situated at 712 Brady Avenue which exceed $1,000. He also has an
interest in rental units situated at 552 College Avenue and 1116-
22 Virginia Avenue. His interest in these rental units exceeds 10
percent and the value of the units exceeds $100,000. These
interests in real property are within 2,500 feet of projects #15,
#16, #17, and #18.

Councilmember Kenni Friedman

Councilmember Friedman has an interest in her residence
located at 508 Andover Lane which exceeds $100,000. Ms. Friedman
uses one room in her residence as an office. Ms. Friedman's
residence is within 2,500 feet of projects #1 and #2.

City Manager Ed Tewes

Mr. Tewes has an interest in his residence situated at 2700
Marboro which presumably exceeds $1,000. This residence is beyond
a radius of 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of project #7.

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 5


The Proposed Projects

     1.  Installation of a traffic signal at the intersection of
McHenry and Levelend Avenues.  The signal will improve traffic
flow along McHenry, a major street, by improving the ability to
make turns on and off Levelend.

     2.  Rehabilitation of a submersible stormwater lift station
at Ashford Court.  There is an existing lift station at that
location which has deteriorated.

     3.  Repaving two existing downtown city parking lots.

     4.  Perform a structural evaluation of the annex to the city
hall, an existing structure.

     5.  Replacing the sewer along Jefferson from the freeway to
Sutter.  This structure is currently overloaded and deteriorated.

     6.  Abatement of asbestos at the Maddux building, a city
owned building which is used as a youth center.

     7.  Installation of a bridge to connect Lakewood Avenue,
which currently terminates at Scenic Avenue, with Lincoln Avenue.
The existing cross section of Lincoln Avenue would be widened to
become a major street with 100-foot right-of-way and 80-foot
travel way from Scenic Avenue to Dry Creek Drive to the south.
The project would create an arterial street extending from
Griggsmore Avenue to Yosemite Boulevard.  Lakewood Avenue will be
widened between Scenic Drive and Laramie Drive.  The bridge is
expected to reduce traffic and congestion by providing a more
direct route across Dry Creek for many residents in the Lincoln-
Lakewood area.  The project will result in additional traffic and
congestion on selected streets in the neighborhood.

     8.  Restriping Scenic Drive between Oakdale and Claus Roads
to maximize existing roadway and permit traffic to move more
freely.

     9.  Restriping El Vista Drive from Scenic to Yosemite
Boulevard to maximize existing roadway to permit traffic to move
more freely.

     10. Addition of low cost traffic improvements at Scenic
Avenue and Oakdale Avenue.

     11. Maintenance overlay of Oakdale Road from Scenic to
Briggsmore to maintain the pavement on an existing street.

     12. Installation of drinking fountains and improvements in
the existing clubhouse at the Creekside Golf Course.

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 6

     13. Completion of McClure House site improvements.  The
building is currently under construction.  The project will
include moving the structure and all site work.

     14. Reconstruction of a parking lot at Beyer Park.

     15. Restriping Tully to add a two-way left turn lane from 9th
Street to Rumble Road.  The project will improve general traffic
flow.

     16. Modification of the existing traffic signal at College
and Orangeburg Avenues to add a left turn arrow.

     17. Construction of a bus shelter on Tully and Coldwell.
This is currently a heavily used bus stop due to its closeness to
the community college.

     18. Abatement of asbestos at the Coldwell city offices.

                              ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest.  The officials on whose behalf you seek our
advice are public officials.  (Section 82048.)

     Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision, within the meaning of Section 87100, if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official, or on a member of the official's
immediate family⁴ or on:

     (b)  Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more.

     (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     Section 87103(a) and (b).

_________________________
     ⁴ An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and
dependent children.  (Section 82029.)

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 7

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision

A public official makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency. (Regulation 18700(b).) Additionally, a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making
any investigation which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the
official or designated employee and the
purpose of which is to influence the
decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any
report, analysis or opinion, orally or in
writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision.

Regulation 18700(c).

With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)

Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the pending
decisions will have a material financial effect on the economic
interests of any of the public officials on whose behalf you seek
our advice, they will not only be required to disqualify
themselves from participating in formal decisions of the city
council which may affect such interests, but they must also
abstain from attempting to influence such decisions by

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 8

communicating with other members of the city council or with city
staff regarding the decisions.

Economic Interests

The public officials have a number of economic interests
which must be examined in order to determine whether the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act require them to disqualify
themselves from participating in governmental decisions regarding
the proposed projects. These economic interests are as follows:
(1) Economic interests in their residences each in excess of
$1,000; (2) Economic interests in rental properties, each in
excess of $1,000; and (3) Economic interests in each of the
tenants in the rental properties which has been a source of income
to the mayor and councilmembers of $250 or more in the twelve
months preceding a governmental decision. If it is reasonably
foreseeable that any of these economic interests will be affected
materially by the pending governmental decisions, the officials'
disqualification will be required unless the "public generally"
exception applies, as discussed below.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 990-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed projects will
have an effect on the economic interests of the officials.
In general, these projects are designed to improve traffic
circulation and the quality of life in the community. For
example, it is reasonably foreseeable that repaving streets,
adding traffic signals and turn lanes, and restricting roads to
improve the flow of traffic will reduce traffic congestion and
improve the appearance of the neighborhoods. These improvements
will foreseeably affect the value of property in the vicinity of
the projects. Thus, the test for foreseeability is met. However,
disqualification is only if a governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on an official's economic
interests.

You have not provided information regarding the tenants
occupying the rental properties in which Mayor Lang and
Councilmembers Bird, Cogdill, Dobbs and Muratore have economic
interests. Each tenant who has been a source of income of $250 or
more in the twelve months preceding a governmental decision is
also an economic interest. Thus, you must determine whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the pending decisions will have a

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 9

material financial effect on these tenants or on former tenants
who may have been sources of income of $250 or more to the public
officials in the twelve months preceding a decision. For your
information, we are enclosing a general discussion of the
application of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act to
sources of income.

Materiality

Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's economic interests are materially affected
by a decision. If the official's economic interests are directly
involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1 provides the
appropriate standards for assessing materiality. However, when an
official's interests in real property are indirectly involved in a
governmental decision, the appropriate standards for determining
materiality are those of Regulation 18702.3. These regulations
are discussed below.

Real property is deemed to be directly involved in a
governmental decision when any of the following applies:

(A) The decision involves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale,
purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion
from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of real property in which
the official has a direct or indirect interest
(other than a leasehold interest) of $1,000 or
more, or a similar decision affecting such
property;

(B) The decision involves the issuance,
denial or revocation of a license, permit or
other land use entitlement authorizing a specific
use or uses of such property;

(C) The decision involves the imposition,
repeal or modification of any taxes or fees
assessed or imposed on such property; or

(D) The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the
preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions; and real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

(E) For purposes of this subdivision, the
terms "zoning" and "rezoning" shall refer to the

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 10

act of establishing or changing the zoning or land
use designation on the subject property, but shall
not refer to an amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as
changes in the uses permitted, or development
standards applicable, within a particular zoning
category) which is applicable to all other
properties designated in that category.

Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).

The facts you have provided for our review appear to indicate
that the public officials' real property interests will not be
affected directly by the projects. When an official's interests
in real property are affected indirectly by a governmental
decision, the appropriate standards for determining the material
financial effect of the decision are those of Regulation 18702.3.
For real property which is situated within a 300-foot radius of
the boundaries or the proposed boundaries of the property which is
the subject of the decision, the effect of the decision is
material unless the decision will have no financial effect upon an
official's real property interests. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(1).)
Accordingly, if any of the officials have interests in real
property within 300 feet of any of the projects, their
disqualification will be required unless the projects will have no
effect on their real property interests.

The effect of a decision is also material as to real property
in which an official has an economic interest if the decision
involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water,
sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property
in which the official has an interest will receive new or
substantially improved services.  (Regulation 18702.3(a)(2).)  It
does not appear that the proposed projects will result in new or
substantially improved services to the public officials'
properties. However, this determination must be made on a case-
by-case basis. We lack sufficient information to proceed with
this analysis.5

You have indicated that several officials have real property
interests beyond 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of the projects.
For these interests in real property the appropriate standards for
determining materiality are those of Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).
This regulation states that the effect of a decision is material
as to real property situated beyond a radius of 300 feet but
within 2,500 feet of the boundaries or the proposed boundaries of
the property which is the subject of the decision, if the decision
will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:

5 The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it issues
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 11

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official has an interest; or

(B) Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period.

This determination must be made on a case-by-case basis.
Accordingly, you must determine whether any of the projects
located beyond a radius of 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of an
official's interests in real property will affect those interests
in the amounts stated above. Factors that must be considered in
making this determination include, but are not limited to:

(1) The proximity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the magnitude of
the proposed project or change in use in
relationship to the property in which the official
has an interest;

(2) Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;

(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood
including, but not limited to, effects on traffic,
view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.

Regulation 18702.3(d).

For example, project #7 involves the construction of a bridge
to connect Lakewood Avenue with Lincoln Avenue. Portions of
Lakewood and Lincoln Avenues will be widened. Councilmember
Bird's residence is within 2,500 feet of this project. If the
project will increase or decrease the value of his residence by
$10,000 or more, he must disqualify himself from participating in
governmental decisions regarding this project.

Councilmember Cogdill's residence is beyond a 2,500 foot
radius of project #7. Thus, the applicable standards for
determining materiality with respect to his interest in his
residence are those of Regulation 18702.3(b). This regulation
states that the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision is not
considered material as to real property situated beyond a 2,500
foot radius of the property which is the subject of the decision
unless:

(1) There are specific circumstances
regarding the decision, its effect, and the nature
of the real property in which the official has an

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 12

interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that
the fair market value or the rental value of the
real property in which the official has an interest
will be affected by the amounts set forth in
subdivisions (a)(3)(A) or (a)(3)(B); and

(2) Either of the following apply:
(A) The effect will not be
substantially the same as the effect upon
at least 25 percent of all the properties
which are within a 2,500 foot radius of
the boundaries of the real property in
which the official has an interest; or

(B) There are not at least 10
properties under separate ownership
within a 2,500 foot radius of the
property in which the official has an
interest.

You have not provided facts concerning property
ownership in the proximity of this project. Accordingly, we
must leave this determination to you. You must apply the
above standards to determine whether the effect of
governmental decisions regarding this project will have a
material financial effect on the councilmember's residence.

Public Generally

Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if
the effect of a decision is distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally. (Section 87100.) If the decision
does not affect all members of the public in the same manner,
disqualification may be required unless the effect of the
decision on the public official's economic interests is the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.) For purposes of our discussion, the
public is the entire population of the city. (In re Logan
(1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77;
Jorgensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-017.)

It would appear that the "public generally" exception
would not apply to the pending decisions because the pending
decisions will affect residents in the immediate vicinity of
the projects in a manner which is distinguishable from the
effect of the decisions on residents who live at a greater
distance from the projects. Accordingly, the "public
generally" exception is not applicable to the pending
decisions.

Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375
Page 13

We trust this letter adequately responds to your
inquiry. Should you have any further questions regarding
this matter, do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.6

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:bb

Enclosure

6 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.conclusion:
1.
Mayor Richard Lang may participate in decisions to instal
a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and Leveland
Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift station
at Ashford Court unless you determine that decisions regarding
these projects will have a material financial effect on the
mayor’s economic interests.
2.
Councilmembers Bird and Codgill may participate in
decisions to repave city parking lots, perform a structural
evaluation of the annex to the city hail, replace a sewer and
abate asbestos at the Maddux building, install a bridge to connect
Lincoln and Lakewood Avenues, restripe Scenic Drive and El Vista
Drive,
add traffic improvements at Scenic Avenue and Oakdale
Avenue, perform maintenance overlay work on Oakdale Road,
instal
drinking fountains and make improvements in the existing clubhouse
at the Creekside Golf Course,
and complete McClure House site


Our File No.
1—92-369 through 1—92-375
improvements unless you determine that these decisions will have a
material financial effect on their economic interests.
3.
Councilmember Stan Dobb may participate in decisions to
reconstruct a parking lot at Beyer Park and to restripe Scenic
Drive unless you determine that decisions regarding these projects
will have a material financial effect on his economic interests.
4.
Counci].member Frank Muratore may participate in decisions
to restripe Tully, modify the existing traffic signal at College
and Orangeburg Avenues, build a bus shelter on Tully and Coidwell,
and abate asbestos at the Coidwell city offices unless you
determine that decisions regarding these projects will have a
material financial effect on his economic interests.
5.
Councilmember Kenni Friedman may participate in decisions
to instal a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and
Leveland Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift
station at Ashford Court unless you determine that decisions
regarding these projects will have a material financial effect on
her interests in real property.
6.
City Manager Ed Tewes may participate in decisions
regarding construction of a bridge to connect Lincoln and Lakewood
Avenues unless you determine that this project will have a
material financial effect on his interests in real property.
CTS
The City of Modesto is in the process of implementing various
projects.
You seek our advice to determine whether seven public
officials who own real property within the jurisdiction must
disqualify themselves from participating in governmental decisions
regarding these projects.
All of these projects are situated
beyond a radius of 300 feet from the public officials’ interests
in real property.
Interests in real property
Mayor Richard Lang
Mayor Lang has an interest in his residence which exceeds
$100,000.
The mayor also owns two investment properties located
at 2604 and 2613 MacGregor.
His interest in these properties
exceeds $10,000.
These investment properties consist of rental
units.
The mayor’s interests in real property are located within
2,500 feet of projects #1 and #2.
The projects are listed below in numerical order for purposes
of convenience in proceeding with our analysis.


Our File No.
1—92—369 through 1—92—375
Councilmember Charles Bird

He
also has a 1/3 interest in investment property situated at 701
H

The fair market value of this real property exceeds
$100,000.
Mr. Bird’s interests in real property are situated
within 2,500 feet of projects 13,
#4,

and #13.
Mr. Bird’s residence is beyond 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of
project #7.
Councilmember Dave Cogdill
Councilmember Cogdill has an interest greater than $1,000 in

He also has an
interest in investment property situated at 1317 I Street.
His
ownership interest in this property exceeds ten percent and the

Mr. Cogdill’s
interests in real property are situated within 2,500 feet of
projects #3,
#4,
#5,
#6,
#8,
#9,
#10,
#11,
#12,
and #13.
Mr. Cogdill’s residence is beyond a 2,500 foot radius of project
#7.
Councilmeinber Stan Dobbs
Councilmember Dobbs has an interest greater than $1,000 in
his residence situated at 2629 Portsmouth Lane.
The residence is
within a 2,500 foot radius of project #8.
He also has an interest
in investment property situated at 3400 Kingswood Drive #12.
His
interest in this property is greater than 10 percent and the fair
market value of the property is estimated to be between $10,000
and $100,000.
This investment property is within a 2,500 radius
of project #14.
Councilmember Frank Muratore
Councilmember Muratore has an interest in his residence
situated at 712 Brady Avenue which exceed $1,000.
He also has an
interest in rental units situated at 552 College Avenue and 1116-
22 Virginia Avenue.
His interest in these rental units exceeds
10
percent and the value of the units exceeds $100,000.
These
interests in real property are within 2,500 feet of projects #15,
#16,
#17,
and #18.
Councilmember Kenni Friedman
Councilmember Friedman has an interest in her residence
located at 508 Andover Lane which exceeds $100,000.
Ms.
Friedman
uses one room in her residence as an office.
Ms.
Friedman’s
residence is within 2,500 feet of projects #1 and #2.
City Manager Ed Tewes
Mr.
Tewes has an interest in his residence situated at 2700
Marboro which presumably exceeds $1,000.
This residence is beyond
a radius of 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of project #7.


Our through 1—92-375
The ProDosed Prolects
1.
Installation of a traffic signal at the intersection of
McHenry and Leveland Avenues.
The signal will improve traffic
flow along McHenry,
a major street,
by improving the ability to
make turns on and off Leveland.
2.
Rehabilitation of a submersible stormwater lift station
at Ashford Court.
There is an existing lift station at that
location which has deteriorated.
3.
Repaving two existing downtown city parking lots.
4.
Perform a structural evaluation of the annex to the city
hall,
an existing structure.
5.
Replacing the sewer along Jefferson from the freeway to
Sutter.
This structure is currently overloaded and deteriorated.
6.
A.batement of asbestos at the Maddux building,
a city
owned building which is used as a youth center.
7.
Installation of a bridge to connect Lakewood Avenue,
which currently terminates at Scenic Avenue, with Lincoln Avenue.
The existing cross section of Lincoln Avenue would be widened to
become a major street with 100-foot right—of—way and 80-foot
travel way from Scenic Avenue to Dry Creek Drive to the south.
The project would create an arterial street extending from
Griggsmore Avenue to Yosemite Boulevard.
Lakewood Avenue will be
widened between Scenic Drive and Laramie Drive.
The bridge is
expected to reduce traffic and congestion by providing a more
direct route across Dry Creek for many residents in the Lincoln
Lakewood area.
The project will result in additional traffic and
congestion on selected streets in the neighborhood.
8.
Restriping Scenic Drive between Oakdale and Claus Roads
to maximize existing roadway and permit traffic to move more
freely.
9.
Restriping El Vista Drive from Scenic to Yosemite
Boulevard to maximize existing roadway to permit traffic to move
more freely.
10. Addition of low cost traffic improvements at Scenic
Avenue and Oakdale Avenue.
11. Maintenance overlay of Oakdale Road from Scenic to
Briggsmore to maintain the pavement on an existing street.
12. Installation of drinking fountains and improvements in
the existing clubhouse at the Creekside Golf Course.


Our File No.

13.

Street to Rumble Road.
The project will improve general traffic
flow.
16. Modification of the existing traffic signal at College
and Orangeburg Avenues to add a left turn arrow.
17. Construction of a bus shelter on Tully and Coldwell.
This is currently a heavily used bus stop due to its closeness to

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest.
The officials on whose behalf you seek our
advice are public officials.
(Section 82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision, within the meaning of Section 87100, if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official’s
immediate family
4 or on:
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 87103(a)
and (b).
An official’s “immediate family” are his or her spouse and
dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


Our File No.
1—92-369 through 1—92-375
Makina. Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision
A public official makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official votes on a matter,
commits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency.
(Regulation 18700(b).)
Additionally,
a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:
(1)
Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review,
by:
(A)
Conducting research or making
any investigation which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the
official or designated employee and the
purpose of which is to influence the
decision; or
(B)
Preparing or presenting any
report, analysis or opinion, orally or in
writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision.
Regulation 18700(c).
With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official’s agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency,
an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before,
or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client,
or customer.
(Regulation 18700.1.)
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the pending
decisions will have a material financial effect on the economic
interests of any of the public officials on whose behalf you seek
our advice, they will not only be required to disqualify
themselves from participating in formal decisions of the city
council which may affect such interests, but they must also
abstain from attempting to influence such decisions by


Our File No.
1—92-369 through 1—92—375
communicating with other members of the city council or with city
staff regarding the decisions.
Economic Interests
The public officials have a number of economic interests
which must be examined in order to determine whether the conflict—
of-interest provisions of the Act require them to disqualify
themselves from participating in governmental decisions regarding
the proposed projects.
These economic interests are as follows:
(1)
Economic interests in their residences each in excess of
$1,000;
(2)
Economic interests in rental properties,
each in
excess of $1,000;
and
(3)
Economic interests in each of the
tenants in the rental properties which has been a source of income
to the mayor and councilmembers of $250 or more in the twelve
months preceding a governmental decision.
If it is reasonably
foreseeable that
of these economic interests will be affected
materially by the pending governmental decisions, the officials’
disqualification will be required unless the “public generally”
exception applies,
as discussed below.
Foreseeabi 1 itv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Comm.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989—
991; Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.App.3d 817;
In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest.
(Witt v.
Morrow,
supra at 823.)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed projects will
have an effect on the economic interests of the officials.
In general, these projects are designed to improve traffic
circulation and the quality of life in the community.
For
example, it is reasonably foreseeable that repaving streets,
adding traffic signals and turn lanes,
and restriping roads to
improve the flow of traffic will reduce traffic congestion and
improve the appearance of the neighborhoods.
These improvements
will foreseeably affect the value of property in the vicinity of
the projects.
Thus, the test for foreseeability is met.
However,
disqualification is only required if a governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on an official’s economic
interests.
You have not provided information regarding the tenants
occupying the rental properties in which Mayor Lang and
Councilmembers Bird,
Cogdill,
Dobbs and Muratore have economic
interests.
Each tenant who has been a source of income of $250 or
more in the twelve months preceding a governmental decision is
also an economic interest.
Thus,
you must determine whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the pending decisions will have a


Our File No.
1—92—369 through 1—92—375
material financial effect on these tenants or on former tenants
who may have been sources of income of $250 or more to the public
officials in the twelve months preceding a decision.
For your
information,
we are enclosing a general discussion of the
application of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act to
sources of income.
Materiality
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official’s economic interests are materially affected
by a decision.
If the official’s economic interests are directly
involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1 provides the
appropriate standards for assessing materiality.
However,
when an
official’s interests in real property are indirectly involved in a
governmental decision, the appropriate standards for determining
materiality are those of Regulation 18702.3.
These regulations
are discussed below.
Real property is deemed to be directly involved in a
governmental decision when any of the following applies:
(A)
The decision involves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale,
purchase,
or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion
from any city,
county, district or other local
governmental subdivision,
of real property in which
the official has a direct or indirect interest
(other than a leasehold interest)
of $1,000 or
more, or a similar decision affecting such
property;
(B)
The decision involves the issuance,
denial or revocation of a license, permit or
other land use entitlement authorizing a specific
use or uses of such property;
(C)
The decision involves the imposition,
repeal or modification of any taxes or fees
assessed or imposed on such property; or
(D)
The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the
preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions; and real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the redevelopment area.
(E)
For purposes of this subdivision, the
terms “zoning” and “rezoning” shall refer to the


Our File No.
1—92—369 through 1—92—375
act of establishing or changing the zoning or land
use designation on the subject property, but shall
not refer to an amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as
changes in the uses permitted,
or development
standards applicable, within a particular zoning
category) which is applicable to all other
properties designated in that category.
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3).
The facts you have provided’ for our review appear to indicate
that the public officials’ real property interests will not be
affected directly by the projects.
When an official’s interests
in real property are affected indirectly by a governmental
decision, the appropriate standards for determining the material
financial effect of the decision are those of Regulation 18702.3.
For real property which is situated within a 300-foot radius of
the boundaries or the proposed boundaries of the property which is
the subject of the decision, the effect of the decision is
material unless the decision will have no financial effect upon an
official’s real property interests.
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (1).)
Accordingly, if any of the officials have interests in real
property within 300 feet of any of the projects, their
disqualification will be required unless the projects will have
effect on their real property interests.
The effect of a decision is also material as to real property
in which an official has an economic interest if the decision
“involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water,
sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities,
and the real property
in which the official has an interest will receive new or
substantially improved services.”
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (2).)
It
does not appear that the proposed projects will result in new or
substantially improved services to the public officials’
properties.
However, this determination must be made on a case—
by—case basis.
We lack sufficient information to proceed with
this analysis.
5
You have indicated that several officials have real property
interests beyond 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of the projects.
For these interests in real property the appropriate standards for
determining materiality are those of Regulation 18702.3(a) (3).
This regulation states that the effect of a decision is material
as to real property situated beyond a radius of 300 feet but
within 2,500 feet of the boundaries or the proposed boundaries of
the property which is the subject of the decision, if the decision
will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it issues
advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 71.)


Our File No.
1—92—369 through 1—92-375
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official has an interest; or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period.
This determination must be made on a case—by—case basis.
Accordingly, you must determine whether any of the projects
located beyond a radius of 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of an
official’s interests in real property will affect those interests
in the amounts stated above.
Factors that must be considered in
making this determination include, but are not limited to:
(1)
The proximity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the magnitude of
the proposed project or change in use in
relationship to the property in which the official
has an interest;
(2)
Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;
(3)
In addition to the foregoing,
in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood
including,
but not limited to, effects on traffic,
view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions,
or similar traits of the neighborhood.
Regulation 18702.3(d).
For example, project #7 involves the construction of a bridge
to connect Lakewood Avenue with Lincoln Avenue.
Portions of
Lakewood and Lincoln Avenues will be widened.
Councilmember
Bird’s residence is within 2,500 feet of this project.
If the
project will increase or decrease the value of his residence by
$10,000 or more,
he must disqualify himself from participating in
governmental decisions regarding this project.
Councilmember Cogdill’s residence is beyond a 2,500 foot
radius of project #7.
Thus, the applicable standards for
determining materiality with respect to his interest in his
residence are those of Regulation 18702.3(b).
This regulation
states that the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision is not
considered material as to real property situated beyond a 2,500
foot radius of the property which is the subject of the decision
unless:
(1)
There are specific circumstances
regarding the decision, its effect,
and the nature
of the real property in which the official has an


Our through 1—92-375
interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that
the fair market value or the rental value of the
real property in which the official has an interest
will be affected by the amounts set forth in
subdivisions (a)(3)(A)
or (a)(3)(B); and
(2)
Either of the following apply:
(A)
The effect will not be
substantially the same as the effect upon
at least 25 percent of all the properties
which are within a 2,500 foot radius of
the boundaries of the real property in
which the official has an interest; or
(B)
There are not at least 10
properties under separate ownership
within a 2,500 foot radius of the
property in which the official has an
interest.
You have not provided facts concerning property
ownership in the proximity of this project.
Accordingly,
we
must leave this determination to you.
You must apply the
above standards to determine whether the effect of
governmental decisions regarding this project will have a
material financial effect on the councilmember’s residence.
Public Generally
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if
the effect of a decision is distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally.
(Section 87100.)
If the decision
does not affect all members of the public in the same manner,
disqualification may be required unless the effect of the
decision on the public official’s economic interests is the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.)
For purposes of our discussion, the
public is the entire population of the city.
(In re Legan
(1985)
9 FPPC Ops.
1; In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77;
Jorgensen Advice Letter,
No. A-90-0l7.)
It would appear that the “public generally” exception
would not apply to the pending decisions because the pending
decisions will affect residents in the immediate vicinity of
the projects in a manner which is distinguishable from the
effect of the decisions on residents who live at a greater
distance from the projects.
Accordingly, the “public
generally” exception is not applicable to the pending
decisions.


Our File No.
1—92-369 through 1—92—375
We trust this letter adequately responds to your
inquiry.
Should you have any fu.rther questions regarding
this matter,
do not hesitate to call me at
(916)
322_5901.6

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under the conflict—of-interest provisions of the Act,
may the
public officials on whose behalf you seek our advice participate
in governmental decisions regarding projects situated beyond a
radius of 300 feet from their interests in real property?
,
.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—92—369 through 1—92-375
1.
May Mayor Richard Lang participate in decisions to instal
a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and Leveland
Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift station
at Ashford Court?
2.
May Councilmembers Bird and Codgill participate in
decisions to repave city parking lots, perform a structural
evaluation of the annex to the city hall, replace a sewer and
abate asbestos at the Maddux building?
May they participate in
decisions to install a bridge to connect Lincoln and Lakewood
Avenues, restripe Scenic Drive and El Vista Drive,
and add traffic
improvements at Scenic Avenue and Oakdale Avenue?
May they
participate in decisions to perform maintenance overlay work on
Oakdale Road, instal drinking fountains and make improvements in
the existing clubhouse at the Creekside Golf Course,
and complete
McClure House site improvements?
3.
May Councilmember Stan Dobb participate in decisions to
reconstruct a parking lot at Beyer Park and to restripe Scenic
Drive?
4.
May Councilmember Frank Muratore participate in decisions
to restripe Tully, modify the existing traffic signal at College
and Orangeburg Avenues, build a bus shelter on Tully and Coidwell,
and abate asbestos at the Coidwell city offices?
5.
May Councilmember Kenni Friedman participate in decisions
to instal a traffic signal at the intersection of Mdllenry and
Leveland Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift
station at Ashford Court?
6.
May City Manager Ed Tewes participate in decisions
regarding construction of a bridge to connect Lincoln and La]cewood
Avenues?

CONCLUSION: 1.
Mayor Richard Lang may participate in decisions to instal
a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and Leveland
Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift station
at Ashford Court unless you determine that decisions regarding
these projects will have a material financial effect on the
mayor’s economic interests.
2.
Councilmembers Bird and Codgill may participate in
decisions to repave city parking lots, perform a structural
evaluation of the annex to the city hail, replace a sewer and
abate asbestos at the Maddux building, install a bridge to connect
Lincoln and Lakewood Avenues, restripe Scenic Drive and El Vista
Drive,
add traffic improvements at Scenic Avenue and Oakdale
Avenue, perform maintenance overlay work on Oakdale Road,
instal
drinking fountains and make improvements in the existing clubhouse
at the Creekside Golf Course,
and complete McClure House site


Our File No.
1—92-369 through 1—92-375
improvements unless you determine that these decisions will have a
material financial effect on their economic interests.
3.
Councilmember Stan Dobb may participate in decisions to
reconstruct a parking lot at Beyer Park and to restripe Scenic
Drive unless you determine that decisions regarding these projects
will have a material financial effect on his economic interests.
4.
Counci].member Frank Muratore may participate in decisions
to restripe Tully, modify the existing traffic signal at College
and Orangeburg Avenues, build a bus shelter on Tully and Coidwell,
and abate asbestos at the Coidwell city offices unless you
determine that decisions regarding these projects will have a
material financial effect on his economic interests.
5.
Councilmember Kenni Friedman may participate in decisions
to instal a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and
Leveland Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift
station at Ashford Court unless you determine that decisions
regarding these projects will have a material financial effect on
her interests in real property.
6.
City Manager Ed Tewes may participate in decisions
regarding construction of a bridge to connect Lincoln and Lakewood
Avenues unless you determine that this project will have a
material financial effect on his interests in real property.
CTS
The City of Modesto is in the process of implementing various
projects.
You seek our advice to determine whether seven public
officials who own real property within the jurisdiction must
disqualify themselves from participating in governmental decisions
regarding these projects.
All of these projects are situated
beyond a radius of 300 feet from the public officials’ interests
in real property.
Interests in real property
Mayor Richard Lang

ownership interest in this property exceeds ten percent and the

interests in real property are situated within 2,500 feet of

#4,
#5,

#12,

Councilmember Dobbs has an interest greater than $1,000 in

The residence is
within a 2,500 foot radius of project #8.

percent and the value of the units exceeds $100,000.

interests in real property are within 2,500 feet of projects #15,
#16,
#17,
and #18.
Councilmember Kenni Friedman
Councilmember Friedman has an interest in her residence
located at 508 Andover Lane which exceeds $100,000.
Ms.
Friedman
uses one room in her residence as an office.
Ms.
Friedman’s
residence is within 2,500 feet of projects #1 and #2.
City Manager Ed Tewes
Mr.
Tewes has an interest in his residence situated at 2700
Marboro which presumably exceeds $1,000.
This residence is beyond
a radius of 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of project #7.


Our through 1—92-375
The ProDosed Prolects
1.
Installation of a traffic signal at the intersection of
McHenry and Leveland Avenues.
The signal will improve traffic
flow along McHenry,
a major street,
by improving the ability to
make turns on and off Leveland.
2.
Rehabilitation of a submersible stormwater lift station
at Ashford Court.
There is an existing lift station at that
location which has deteriorated.
3.
Repaving two existing downtown city parking lots.
4.
Perform a structural evaluation of the annex to the city
hall,
an existing structure.
5.
Replacing the sewer along Jefferson from the freeway to
Sutter.
This structure is currently overloaded and deteriorated.
6.
A.batement of asbestos at the Maddux building,
a city
owned building which is used as a youth center.
7.
Installation of a bridge to connect Lakewood Avenue,
which currently terminates at Scenic Avenue, with Lincoln Avenue.
The existing cross section of Lincoln Avenue would be widened to
become a major street with 100-foot right—of—way and 80-foot
travel way from Scenic Avenue to Dry Creek Drive to the south.
The project would create an arterial street extending from
Griggsmore Avenue to Yosemite Boulevard.
Lakewood Avenue will be
widened between Scenic Drive and Laramie Drive.
The bridge is
expected to reduce traffic and congestion by providing a more
direct route across Dry Creek for many residents in the Lincoln
Lakewood area.
The project will result in additional traffic and
congestion on selected streets in the neighborhood.
8.
Restriping Scenic Drive between Oakdale and Claus Roads
to maximize existing roadway and permit traffic to move more
freely.
9.
Restriping El Vista Drive from Scenic to Yosemite
Boulevard to maximize existing roadway to permit traffic to move
more freely.
10. Addition of low cost traffic improvements at Scenic
Avenue and Oakdale Avenue.
11. Maintenance overlay of Oakdale Road from Scenic to
Briggsmore to maintain the pavement on an existing street.
12. Installation of drinking fountains and improvements in
the existing clubhouse at the Creekside Golf Course.


Our File No.
1—92—369 through 1—92—375
13.
Completion of Mcclure Rouse site improvements.
The
building is currently under construction.
The project will
include moving the structure and all site work.
14. Reconstruction of a parking lot at Beyer Park.
15. Restriping Tully to add a two-way left turn lane from 9th
Street to Rumble Road.
The project will improve general traffic
flow.
16. Modification of the existing traffic signal at College
and Orangeburg Avenues to add a left turn arrow.
17. Construction of a bus shelter on Tully and Coldwell.
This is currently a heavily used bus stop due to its closeness to
the community college.
18. Abatement of asbestos at the Coldwell city offices.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission July 15, 1992 Laury L. Dowd Senior Deputy City Attorney 801 11th Street P.O. Box 642 Modesto, CA 95353 Re: Your Request for Advice Our Files Nos. I-92-369 through I-92-375 Dear Ms. Dowd: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the duties and responsibilities of City of Modesto Mayor Richard Lang, Councilmembers Ken Friedman, Frank Muratore, Charles Bird, David Cogdill and Stan Dobbs, and City Manager Ed Tewes under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Your letter advises not seek advice regarding a specific pending governmental decision but rather seeks guidance with respect to various pending decisions. Accordingly, we treat your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.2 QUESTIONS Under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, may the public officials on whose behalf you seek our advice participate in governmental decisions regarding projects situated beyond a radius of 300 feet from their interests in real property? 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. I-92-369 through I-92-375 Page 2 1. May Mayor Richard Lang participate in decisions to instal a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and Leveland Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift station at Ashford Court? 2. May Councilmembers Bird and Codgill participate in decisions to repair city parking lots, perform a structural evaluation of the annex to the city hall, replace a sewer and abate asbestos at the Maddux building? May they participate in decisions to install a bridge to connect Lincoln and Lakewood Avenues, restripe Scenic Drive and El Vista Drive, and add traffic improvements at Scenic Avenue and Oakdale Avenue? May they participate in decisions to perform maintenance overlay work on Oakdale Road, install drinking fountains and make improvements in the existing clubhouse at the Creekside Golf Course, and complete McClure House site improvements? 3. May Councilmember Stan Dobb participate in decisions to reconstruct a parking lot at Beyer Park and to restripe Scenic Drive? 4. May Councilmember Frank Muratore participate in decisions to restripe Tully, modify the existing traffic signal at College and Orangeburg Avenues, build a bus shelter on Tully and Coldwell, and abate asbestos at the Coldwell city offices? 5. May Councilmember Kenni Friedman participate in decisions to instal a traffic signal at the intersection of McHenry and Leveland Avenues and to rehabilitate a submersible stormwater lift station at Ashford Court? 6. May City Manager Ed Tewes participate in decisions regarding construction of a bridge to connect Lincoln and Lakewood
